metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the env var name conflict: pick one canonical name (`REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` is consistent with the `REBAR_LLM_*` namespace) and update both the Scope section and AC4 to match.
findings.finding: The env var name for the drain opt-out is stated as `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` in the body/Scope section but as `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN` in AC4 — a direct conflicting signal that makes the acceptance criterion unverifiable as written.
findings.location: AC4 vs Scope section: 'env `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN`' vs AC4 'env `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` everywhere (matches the `[tool.rebar.llm]` config layer and the `REBAR_LLM_*` env-var pattern). Update AC4 to read: '`overlap_drain=off` (env `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN`) disables the opportunistic drain entirely'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named test function for each sub-claim in AC2: e.g. `test_drain_survives_parent_exit` (assert child PID still running after parent returns), `test_gate_noop_on_empty_queue`, and `test_gate_latency_under_budget`; cite each by name in AC2.
findings.finding: AC2 bundles three distinct verifiable claims — (a) detached process survives parent exit, (b) gate no-ops with no spawn when queue is empty, (c) gate latency ≤ DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS — but only (b) and (c) have any test hint; (a) 'survives parent exit' has no named test or proving command.
findings.location: AC2: 'An ordinary invocation opportunistically detaches a drain that survives parent exit; when nothing is soaked the gate no-ops with no spawn (assert via test); the foreground command's added latency is within `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS` (default 20ms)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the env var name for the opt-out flag to a single canonical value across the What section and AC #4 (either `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` or `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`, consistently).
findings.finding: The env var name for the opt-out flag is stated as `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` in the 'What' section but as `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN` in AC #4, creating a cross-section contradiction that would send the implementer in two directions.
findings.location: What section ('Opt-out via the TYPED config `overlap_drain = "off"` (env `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN`)') vs AC #4 ('env `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`')
findings.suggested_fix: Choose one name — the 'What' section's `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` is consistent with the `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` namespace stated in Scope — and update AC #4 to match.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `overlap_enabled` (Scope/VerifyConfig) and `overlap_drain` (What/LLMConfig) are the same flag or separate controls, reconcile naming across all sections, and add an AC that verifies the `overlap_enabled` flag's config path and behavior if it is a distinct deliverable.
findings.finding: The Scope section introduces a distinct `overlap_enabled` flag in `VerifyConfig [verify]` as the gate enable control, but no AC verifies that this flag exists, its config path, or its behavior; the What section only describes `overlap_drain` as the opt-out, leaving the relationship between `overlap_drain` and `overlap_enabled` ambiguous and uncovered by any criterion.
findings.location: Scope section ('Only the gate ENABLE flag `overlap_enabled` lives in `VerifyConfig` `[verify]`') vs What section (only mentions `overlap_drain`) vs AC #4 (only mentions `overlap_drain`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` to the Scope edit targets and specify the `if argv and argv[0] == 'enrich':` intercept block that imports and calls the new handler from `_llm_commands.py`.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` is an unenumerated edit target. Every `rebar <subcommand>` requires an explicit intercept block in `main()` in that file (e.g. `if argv and argv[0] == "enrich": return _enrich(argv[1:])`). The Scope section lists only `_llm_commands.py` as the CLI edit target, but `__init__.py` owns the top-level dispatch and must import and invoke the new handler.
findings.location: Scope section — 'CLI arm in `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: 'wiring in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py`: add `if argv and argv[0] == "enrich": return _enrich(argv[1:])` intercept (mirroring the `review-plan` arm) and import `_enrich` from `_llm_commands`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/help/enrich.txt` to the Scope edit targets so `known_subcommands()` recognises `enrich` and the `--help` path works.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_cli/help/enrich.txt` is an unenumerated edit target. `known_subcommands()` in `_help.py` discovers subcommands purely by scanning `.txt` files in `src/rebar/_cli/help/`; without this file the new subcommand is treated as unknown and rejected before dispatch.
findings.location: Scope section — CLI arm description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete file(s) where `maybe_drain()` is called (e.g. `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` or a specific command module) and add them to the Scope edit targets.
findings.finding: The `maybe_drain()` call-site is underspecified. The plan says the hook is 'attached to the same post-write path as push (or to `review_plan`/`claim`)' but names no concrete edit target. The actual post-write callsite is `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py::write_and_push` (which already calls `push.push_tickets_branch`) or the per-command write paths. Neither `event_append.py` nor any specific calling module appears in the Scope section.
findings.location: Scope section — 'opportunistic hook attached to the same post-write path as push (or to `review_plan`/`claim`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the opt-out env var name: pick one of `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` (consistent with the `REBAR_LLM_*` namespace for LLM config) or `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN` and use it consistently in the What section, Scope section, and AC4.
findings.finding: The env-var name for the opt-out flag is contradicted between the plan body and AC4. The body says `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN`; AC4 says `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`. These are two different env var names.
findings.location: What section ('env `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN`') vs AC4 ('env `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`')
findings.suggested_fix: Use `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` throughout (consistent with the LLMConfig `REBAR_LLM_*` namespace established in `config.py`) and update AC4 accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` to the Scope edit targets, specifying that both the `VerifyConfig` dataclass field and the `_SECTIONS['verify']` coercion lambda for `overlap_enabled` must be added.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` is an unenumerated edit target. The plan places `overlap_enabled` in `VerifyConfig` (section `[verify]`), but `VerifyConfig` lives in `_config_schema.py` along with its `_SECTIONS` coercion table. Both the dataclass field and the `_SECTIONS['verify']` lambda must be added; neither that file nor its coercion table appear in the Scope section.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Only the gate ENABLE flag `overlap_enabled` lives in `VerifyConfig` `[verify]`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the opt-out env var name: change AC4's `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN` to `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` to match the LLMConfig convention stated in the 'What' section.
findings.finding: AC4 uses the env var name `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`, but the 'What' section places `overlap_drain` in LLMConfig under `[tool.rebar.llm]` and follows the `REBAR_LLM_*` naming convention — making the correct env var `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN`. The AC therefore specifies the wrong env var name, so a test that validates the opt-out by setting `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN` would pass even if the implementation wires `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN`.
findings.location: AC4 vs. 'What' section (overlap_drain config placement)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, replace `env \`REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN\`` with `env \`REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN\`` to match the `REBAR_LLM_*` prefix pattern used by all other LLMConfig env keys.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the assert_gated interaction: the plan must specify that either (a) the drainer bootstraps a `gate_session()` / `gate_read_root()` context before calling enrich, or (b) S1's enrich op is designed as a non-tool-using agent (model_override path) that bypasses the safeguard, or (c) the detached child sets REBAR_GATE_ALLOW_UNGATED — and add a corresponding test assertion that the drain child does not raise on assert_gated.
findings.finding: The detached drain child process loses all ContextVar state from the parent. When the child calls `enrich` (S1), the LLM runner's `assert_gated()` check (runner.py:203-206) fires — because `_in_gate_session` ContextVar defaults to False in a new process — and raises RuntimeError, killing the drain silently. The plan does not require S1 to bootstrap a gate session for the standalone drain context, nor does it specify passing REBAR_GATE_ALLOW_UNGATED, nor does it state that the drainer must call `gate_session()` / `gate_read_root()` before invoking enrich.
findings.location: 'What' section — drainer loop; src/rebar/llm/runner.py:203-206; src/rebar/llm/gate_source.py:104-117
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' → drainer loop description, add: 'The drainer wraps each enrich call inside `gate_read_root(resolve_handle(source="local"))` (or a bare `gate_session()` for a non-file-reading enrich) so the assert_gated safeguard passes in the orphaned child process.' Update the unit test list to cover this: '--once in the detached-child context does not raise assert_gated.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete valid value vocabulary for `overlap_drain` (e.g. `{"on", "off"}` or `{"always", "async", "off"}`) alongside the `_as_choice` coercer entry, mirroring how `sync.push` is specified in `_config_schema.py`.
findings.finding: The `overlap_drain` config key's full value vocabulary is never defined: only `"off"` is named as the opt-out. The explicit analogy `sync.push` is a confirmed 3-value enum (`always | async | off`, `_config_schema.py:449`); an executor implementing the typed config layer cannot infer the remaining valid values (e.g. whether `"on"` or `"async"` exist) and has no `_as_choice` call-site to write.
findings.location: What section ('Opt-out via the TYPED config `overlap_drain = "off"'); Scope section ('overlap_drain in the rebar.llm config layer'); AC #4
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: '`overlap_drain` is a 2-value choice key `{"on", "off"}` (default `"on"`); registered in `LLMConfig` as `_llm_str(…, default="on")` and gated with `if cfg.overlap_drain == "off": return`.' If async-detach is a distinct mode, spell out the 3-value set explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the env var name for `overlap_drain` to a single canonical value across all three locations (What, Scope, AC #4) — either `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` (consistent with `REBAR_LLM_*` namespace used by all other LLMConfig env vars) or `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`.
findings.finding: The env var name for the `overlap_drain` opt-out contradicts itself across plan sections: the 'What' bullet and Scope section both state `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN`, but AC #4 names it `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`. These are two different env vars; an executor reading AC #4 as the authoritative spec will wire a different env var than one reading the body.
findings.location: What section ('env `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN`'); Scope section ('env `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*`'); AC #4 ('env `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`')
findings.suggested_fix: Use `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` throughout (consistent with the `REBAR_LLM_*` env namespace already established in `LLMConfig.from_env`); update AC #4 to match.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling policy for `enrich` (S1) failures in the drainer loop: e.g. 'on exception: log to `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log`, skip this ticket (do NOT append DONE_ENRICH, leave claimable for next run via S3 lease expiry), continue to next entry in batch' — and add a corresponding unit test case.
findings.finding: No fallback policy is specified for when the `enrich` (S1) operation fails mid-drain (exception, timeout, or non-success return). The drainer loop specifies 'run `enrich` (S1), write `TICKET_DIGEST` (S2), append `DONE_ENRICH`' as a linear sequence but gives no error-handling rule: the executor cannot determine whether to skip-and-continue (process next ticket in batch), re-queue (re-append ENQUEUE), abort the batch, or leave the ticket in `claimed` state. The drain runs detached with no parent to catch unhandled exceptions.
findings.location: What section ('drainer loop: … run `enrich` (S1), write `TICKET_DIGEST` (S2), append `DONE_ENRICH`'); Testing section (no failure-path test listed)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the opt-out env var name: AC4 says `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN` but the body and Scope say `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` — pick one canonical name and apply it consistently across all ACs, the 'What' section, and the Scope section.
findings.finding: AC4 names the opt-out env var as `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`, but the body ('What' bullet) and Scope section both specify `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN`. This internal contradiction means the agent will implement inconsistent env var names across the codebase.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC4 vs. 'What' bullet 5 and Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Use `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` everywhere (consistent with the `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` namespace declared in Scope), and update AC4 accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the opt-out env var name: pick ONE canonical name (`REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` to match LLMConfig convention), use it consistently in the What section, AC4, the `LLMConfig.from_env` registration, and the unit test.
findings.finding: The plan names the opt-out env var as `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` in the What section but as `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN` (missing the `LLM_` infix) in AC4. Since `overlap_drain` is specified as an `LLMConfig` key whose env vars follow the `REBAR_LLM_<KEY>` convention, one of the two names is wrong — and the AC4 test will assert the wrong variable name, causing a silent mis-opt-out at runtime.
findings.location: What section (`env REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN`) vs AC4 (`env REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`)
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` everywhere (aligns with LLMConfig's `REBAR_LLM_<KEY>` pattern). Update AC4 to read: '`overlap_drain=off` (env `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN`) disables the opportunistic drain entirely'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a claim-recovery contract: either a TTL on claimed state (e.g., claim older than N minutes reverts to pending on next `pending_enrichment()` call) or an explicit unclaim-on-exception path in the drainer loop, so a crashed child does not permanently strand tickets.
findings.finding: When the detached drain claims a ticket but `enrich` (S1) throws or the child process is killed before writing DONE_ENRICH, the ticket is permanently stuck in 'claimed' state with no recovery path specified in this story.
findings.location: What section: 'The drainer loop: … claim one (locked read-check-append, S3), run enrich (S1), write TICKET_DIGEST (S2), append DONE_ENRICH'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the drainer loop spec: wrap the enrich+write block in try/except; on exception, append a CLAIM_FAILED event (or rely on a TTL in S3 that auto-reverts claimed→pending after a configurable timeout, default 10 min). Document which mechanism S3 provides and which this story relies on.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test for enrich-op failure mid-drain-batch: specify and test whether the drainer skips-and-logs (continuing to remaining tickets), re-queues the failed entry, or halts — and assert the ticket does not remain permanently stuck in 'claimed' state.
findings.finding: The drain loop has no specified test for enrich-op failure mid-batch: when S1's enrich throws an exception for one ticket, the plan does not specify whether the drainer skips-and-continues, halts, or re-queues, and no test exercises this path. This is a new user-facing flow (the drain loop) with a missing failure path that could cause silent ticket loss or a stalled drain.
findings.location: Testing section / `tests/unit/test_enrich_drain.py` / drainer loop spec in What
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_enrich_drain.py`, add `test_drain_enrich_error_skips_and_continues`: configure FakeRunner to raise on the second ticket, assert the drain processes remaining tickets, the failed ticket reverts to unclaimed (or is logged as failed), and exit code is still 0. Document the error-handling contract in the drainer loop spec in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a claim lease/TTL and expiry reaper: CLAIM_ENRICH records must carry a TTL so that a future drain run (or `pending_enrichment`) can reclaim tickets whose claim has expired without a matching DONE_ENRICH, making the drain loop fully resumable after a mid-run crash.
findings.finding: The drain loop is not fully resumable after a mid-run crash. If the detached child is killed (OOM, SIGKILL, unhandled exception) after appending CLAIM_ENRICH but before appending DONE_ENRICH, the ticket is permanently stuck in 'claimed' state. No lease TTL, expiry, or re-queue path is defined in this plan. The plan's idempotency claim ('a re-run overwrites by content-hash') applies only to the TICKET_DIGEST write step, not to the CLAIM record itself.
findings.location: What section — drainer loop: 'claim one (locked read-check-append, S3), run enrich (S1), write TICKET_DIGEST (S2), append DONE_ENRICH'; Testing section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) that verifies the log-file behavior: the detached child writes stderr to `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log` (not DEVNULL), confirmed by `--once` triggering a logged error and asserting the file is written.
findings.finding: The observability guarantee — stderr goes to a rotating log file (NOT DEVNULL) and the log is `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log` git-ignored — is a stated commitment in the What section but has no corresponding AC or proving command, making it unverifiable.
findings.location: What section: 'the detached child does NOT send stderr to DEVNULL — it appends to a rotating log `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log`'; no AC covers this

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike or integration fixture that measures `pending_enrichment()` latency against the real S3 implementation before committing to the 20ms budget, or document the S3 contract requirement (e.g., 'must complete in <5ms for empty queue') in the interface spec.
findings.finding: The 20ms DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS default is asserted without a measurement plan or spike against the actual S3/queue implementation (prerequisite story S3). The latency of `pending_enrichment(now)` depends entirely on how S3 implements its 'soaked+unclaimed' check — which may involve file I/O, DB read, or in-memory scan — none of which is contracted here. The AC states 'assert via test' but the unit test will use a FakeRunner, not the real S3 implementation, so the 20ms claim is empirically unvalidated before full build-out.
findings.location: AC line 2 / What section: 'gate latency ~0 when nothing soaked'; 'foreground command's added latency is within DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS (default 20ms)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the S3 interface spec (or as an AC on S3's story), add: 'pending_enrichment() MUST return in <5ms for the empty/all-claimed case (fast-path, no I/O if cache-warm).' Then add an integration test (not FakeRunner) that asserts this before wiring the gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact call site(s) for `maybe_drain()` — either the same post-write hook as `push.py` or named CLI commands (`review_plan`/`claim`) — and remove the 'or' placeholder.
findings.finding: The hook attachment point for `maybe_drain()` is left as an unresolved 'or': 'the same post-write path as push (or to `review_plan`/`claim`)' — this defers a real design choice to the executor.
findings.location: Scope section: 'the opportunistic hook attached to the same post-write path as push (or to `review_plan`/`claim`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the lock file path, its format (e.g., PID-stamped), and a stale-lock detection policy (e.g., check if the owning PID is still alive; if not, reclaim the lock) so a crashed drain does not permanently block future drains.
findings.finding: The lock file lifecycle is unspecified in this plan: who creates it, what its path is, and — critically — what happens to a stale lock left by a killed/crashed drain process.
findings.location: What section: 'lock-held → silent skip (exit 0, logged)' (Testing); AC #3
findings.suggested_fix: Use a PID-file lock at `.rebar/locks/enrich-drain.lock` containing the owner PID; on acquisition attempt, if the file exists but the PID is not running, treat it as stale and overwrite. Add a test for the stale-lock recovery path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback schema state assertion: specify what the event store looks like after a binary rollback (CLAIM-without-DONE orphans, absent digest keys), provide a compensating-forward path (e.g., lease expiry reaper that works on both old and new binaries), and attach an explicit `DATA LOSS RISK` note if any state is irrecoverable.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new persisted event types (CLAIM_ENRICH, DONE_ENRICH, TICKET_DIGEST) into the shared git-backed event store but never asserts the post-rollback schema STATE. On rollback to a pre-drain binary, CLAIM events without matching DONE events remain as permanent durable orphans — the old binary has no lease reaper — and digest-record events land on disk but are silently absent from the older client's compiled state. Per T11, 'rollback exits 0' is insufficient; the plan must assert the store state after rollback and provide a compensating-forward path or explicit DATA LOSS RISK note.
findings.location: Scope section; AC3 ('concurrent drains do not double-process'); What section — drainer loop

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the rotation mechanism and policy for `enrich-drain.log` (e.g., `RotatingFileHandler(maxBytes=1_000_000, backupCount=3)`) so the implementation is unambiguous and the disk-growth behaviour is bounded and verifiable.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'rotating log' for `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log` without specifying the rotation mechanism (e.g., Python's `logging.handlers.RotatingFileHandler`, `TimedRotatingFileHandler`, or manual truncation), leaving it as an unresolved capability claim. The codebase has no existing rotating-log infrastructure, so this cannot be inherited.
findings.location: What section (Observability bullet) / Testing section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Observability bullet: 'rotated via `logging.handlers.RotatingFileHandler(maxBytes=1_000_000, backupCount=3)` — max ~3 MB on disk; no external dependency beyond stdlib'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and cite the specific Python/Windows behavior for `start_new_session=True` vs `DETACHED_PROCESS|CREATE_NO_WINDOW` (e.g., reference CPython source or subprocess docs), and confirm `subprocess.DETACHED_PROCESS` is accessible as a named constant (Python 3.7+ only on Windows) rather than a raw integer.
findings.finding: The plan claims that `start_new_session=True` on Windows maps only to CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP and does NOT orphan-protect, requiring the `DETACHED_PROCESS|CREATE_NO_WINDOW` creationflags branch. This is an unverified capability assertion: the existing codebase (push.py) uses `start_new_session=True` unconditionally on all platforms and has no Windows-specific branch; the plan's claim has never been exercised in rebar and no citation or test evidence is provided.
findings.location: What section (Detach mechanism bullet) / Testing section (Windows detach path)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consolidate all `overlap_*` config keys (both the gate-enable flag and drain-behavior keys) into a single config layer — either all in `VerifyConfig` (matching enable_code_review, remediation_mode precedent) or all in `LLMConfig` — and document the rationale for any intentional split.
findings.finding: The plan distributes the 'overlap' feature's config keys across TWO config layers: `overlap_enabled` in `VerifyConfig` (core `_config_schema.py`, `[tool.rebar][verify]`) and `overlap_drain` / `overlap_*` in `LLMConfig` (optional `rebar.llm`, `[tool.rebar.llm]`). No analogous LLM gate feature uses this split: `enable_code_review`, `progressive_drift_refresh`, `remediation_mode`, `novelty_drop_active`, and `completion_floor_active` are ALL in `VerifyConfig` alone. Adding `overlap_*` to `LLMConfig` while keeping the gate-enable flag in `VerifyConfig` proliferates config surface for one feature across two distinct config sections with no existing precedent.
findings.location: Scope section: 'overlap_drain in the rebar.llm config layer … Only the gate ENABLE flag overlap_enabled lives in VerifyConfig [verify]'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `status` output format (at minimum: human-readable vs. JSON flag), the zero-state behavior (print zeros or a friendly 'no queue yet' message with exit 0), and add a test for the uninitialized-queue case.
findings.finding: The `status` subcommand output format is unspecified, and its behavior when the queue store does not yet exist (first invocation before any enrichment has run) is not defined, leaving a potentially confusing UX cliff.
findings.location: What section: 'status (print pending/claimed/soaking counts)'; AC #5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: `status` prints a fixed-format table (pending: N, claimed: N, soaking: N) to stdout with exit 0; if the queue store does not exist, prints all zeros with a note 'queue not yet initialized'. A `--json` flag emits machine-readable output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'rotating log' spec with a plain append-open (or stderr-to-file redirect at Popen) — confirm whether Python's `RotatingFileHandler` is genuinely needed for a bounded ephemeral subprocess, or whether a simpler file-append (matching the existing logging convention) suffices.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a rotating log file (`.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log`) for the detached drain child, but `RotatingFileHandler` (or any file-rotation mechanism) has zero existing call-sites in `src/rebar`. Rotation is a daemon pattern — appropriate for long-running processes — but the drain child is explicitly bounded (exits after `DRAIN_BATCH` entries). This is a cargo-cult of daemon observability applied to an ephemeral subprocess: the single consumer does not meet the Rule-of-Three threshold, and the existing rebar logging convention (`_logging.py:install_stderr_handler`) uses a stderr `StreamHandler`, never a rotating file.
findings.location: What section: 'appends to a rotating log `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log`'; AC #3: 'logs to `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'appends to a rotating log' with 'appends to `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log`' via a plain `logging.FileHandler` (append mode) or by passing `stderr=open(log_path, 'a')` to Popen — no rotation needed for a process that writes at most DRAIN_BATCH records per invocation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Observability section and in `enrich_drain.py`'s logging setup that the drain log MUST NOT capture raw exception messages containing credential material — either install a log filter that redacts patterns matching API key formats, or document that the child process catches and sanitises exceptions from the LLM client before writing to stderr/the log.
findings.finding: The detached child's stderr is appended to `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log` with no stated requirement to sanitize or redact secrets. If the LLM client (called via S1's `enrich`) raises an exception that includes an API key value or Authorization header in its message/traceback, that secret lands in a persistent, world-readable log file on the developer's machine.
findings.location: Observability section; AC6 ('the child logs errors to a file (never DEVNULL for stderr)')
findings.suggested_fix: In `enrich_drain.py`, wrap the `enrich()` call in a try/except that catches and re-raises a sanitised exception (stripping Authorization headers), or install a `logging.Filter` on the file handler that redacts strings matching the API key pattern before writing. Also set the log file to mode 0o600 on creation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default value of `DRAIN_BATCH` (e.g. `DRAIN_BATCH = 5`) directly in the plan so the executor and test author use the same number.
findings.finding: `DRAIN_BATCH` is referenced in AC1 and the drainer spec but its default value is never stated, making the plan non-self-contained for the executor who must pick a number.
findings.location: What section: 'process UP TO `DRAIN_BATCH` tickets per run'; AC1: 'verified by `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich_drain.py::test_batch_cap`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: '`DRAIN_BATCH` defaults to 5 (overridable via `REBAR_LLM_DRAIN_BATCH` env var)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting (a) the opportunistic detached-drain pattern and its precedents, (b) the Windows vs POSIX detach divergence and why, and (c) the rationale for splitting overlap_enabled (VerifyConfig) vs overlap_* tunables (LLMConfig).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (opportunistic detached child process for async enrichment, cross-platform orphan-process management, rotating log for diagnosability) that crosses component boundaries (CLI, config, queue, LLM, process model) but does not include any ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry capturing the decision and its rationale.
findings.location: Scope section / What section: detach mechanism, log routing, config split between LLMConfig and VerifyConfig

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default value of DRAIN_BATCH (e.g., 3–5 for low cost impact), document its per-invocation LLM-call cost implication in config docs, and add it as a configurable `overlap_drain_batch` key in LLMConfig (analogous to other overlap_* tunables).
findings.finding: DRAIN_BATCH — the per-run cap on LLM enrichment calls — is referenced throughout the plan but its default value is never stated. Each batch item triggers at least one LLM call (via S1 enrich). Without a stated default, the per-invocation API cost is unbounded-in-practice: a user with a large backlog could unknowingly incur dozens of LLM calls on every ordinary rebar invocation.
findings.location: What section: 'process UP TO DRAIN_BATCH tickets per run'; Testing section: '--drain respects DRAIN_BATCH and exits'
findings.suggested_fix: Set DRAIN_BATCH default to a small value (e.g., 3) explicitly in LLMConfig as `overlap_drain_batch: int = 3`, and note in the config docs: 'Each batch slot triggers one LLM enrich call; increase only if you have a large backlog and accept higher per-invocation API cost.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a retry/backoff policy for the `enrich` call inside the drain loop (e.g., up to N retries with exponential backoff on transient errors), and add a test case covering transient failure recovery.
findings.finding: No retry or backoff is specified for transient LLM call failures inside the drain loop; a single transient network error causes the entire batch to fail silently (logged, but no retry), wasting the drain opportunity.
findings.location: What section / Testing section
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that the drainer catches retryable errors (5xx, network timeout) and retries up to `DRAIN_ENRICH_RETRIES` (default 2) with a short backoff before logging and skipping to the next ticket; non-retryable errors (4xx auth) trigger clean no-op with a log line.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a retirement/pruning policy for DONE_ENRICH entries (e.g., entries older than N days are removed on drain runs, or a max-completed-entries cap), or explicitly defer to S3 and document that S3 owns the retention contract.
findings.finding: DONE_ENRICH queue entries have no retirement/cleanup path: successfully processed tickets accumulate in the queue store indefinitely, with no pruning, archival, or size cap specified.
findings.location: Scope section; What section: 'append DONE_ENRICH'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `DONE_ENRICH_RETENTION_DAYS` config key (default e.g. 30) and specify that the drainer prunes entries older than this threshold at the end of each drain run, keeping the queue store bounded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for the VerifyConfig/LLMConfig split: explain why `overlap_enabled` belongs in `[verify]` (e.g., it gates a workflow step like `require_plan_review_for_claim`, not an LLM tunable) vs. why `overlap_drain` belongs in `[tool.rebar.llm]` — or consider consolidating all `overlap_*` keys into one config table.
findings.finding: The plan places `overlap_enabled` in `VerifyConfig` (`[verify]`) and the remaining `overlap_*` keys (including `overlap_drain`) in `LLMConfig` (`[tool.rebar.llm]`), but provides no positive rationale for this split. VerifyConfig owns 'opt-in gate behaviors that control whether a gate even runs' while LLMConfig owns 'LLM/prompt/scoring tunables' — and an enable flag for a background enrichment job sits awkwardly in a verify config. An operator consulting `[verify]` for drain behavior will not find it; one consulting `[tool.rebar.llm]` will find the drain flag but not the master enable.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Only the gate ENABLE flag `overlap_enabled` lives in `VerifyConfig` `[verify]`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the initial corpus backfill is batched and throttled: add a configurable rate-limit (e.g., max N LLM calls per minute) and/or a one-time seed command that pages through pre-existing tickets in bounded batches, so the initial enrichment does not saturate the LLM API or produce a silently incomplete corpus.
findings.finding: No batching or throttling plan is specified for the initial seeding of pre-existing tickets. On first deploy, all tickets that pass the soak window become eligible simultaneously; the plan describes only ongoing bounded-per-run drain (DRAIN_BATCH) but provides no seed-phase rate limit, cost cap, or staged rollout for the existing corpus.
findings.location: What section — drainer loop; AC1 ('processes up to DRAIN_BATCH soaked+unclaimed entries'); Testing section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the Windows `creationflags` constant must be accessed as `getattr(subprocess, 'DETACHED_PROCESS', 0x8) | getattr(subprocess, 'CREATE_NO_WINDOW', 0x08000000)` (or equivalent `if os.name == 'nt':` guard with raw integers) so the module is safely importable on POSIX.
findings.finding: The Windows detach path uses `creationflags=DETACHED_PROCESS|CREATE_NO_WINDOW`, but `subprocess.DETACHED_PROCESS` is not exposed as a named constant in Python's `subprocess` module on all platforms (it is documented but absent from `subprocess` on non-Windows builds). The plan does not specify whether to use the named constant or the raw integer literals (`0x00000008 | 0x08000000`), leaving the implementation to potentially import a non-existent attribute on POSIX CI runners that execute the Windows branch mock.
findings.location: What section — 'Windows uses `creationflags=DETACHED_PROCESS|CREATE_NO_WINDOW`'; Testing — 'Windows detach path unit-tested via os.name branch (mock Popen kwargs)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope or What section, add: 'Use raw integer literals (`0x00000008 | 0x08000000`) or `getattr` guards for `DETACHED_PROCESS`/`CREATE_NO_WINDOW` — these constants exist only on Windows builds of `subprocess`; referencing them at module level on POSIX raises `AttributeError`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete default value for `DRAIN_BATCH` (e.g. `DRAIN_BATCH = 10`) and specify whether it is user-configurable (via `overlap.drain_batch` / `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN_BATCH`); the `test_batch_cap` test must be able to assert against a known value.
findings.finding: `DRAIN_BATCH` is referenced throughout the plan (AC #1, drainer loop, testing section) as a named constant that bounds per-run work, but no default value is ever stated. An executor writing `enrich_drain.py` must invent an integer with no guidance.
findings.location: What section ('process UP TO `DRAIN_BATCH` tickets per run'); AC #1 ('verified by `pytest … ::test_batch_cap`'); Testing section ('`--drain` respects `DRAIN_BATCH` and exits')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: '`DRAIN_BATCH = 10` (module-level constant in `enrich_drain.py`; configurable via `overlap_drain_batch` in `LLMConfig` / env `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN_BATCH`; `test_batch_cap` monkeypatches it to 2).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the decision rule for why `overlap_enabled` belongs in `VerifyConfig` rather than `LLMConfig` alongside the other `overlap_*` keys, OR consolidate all `overlap_*` keys into one config layer and document the rationale.
findings.finding: The plan splits `overlap_enabled` (the gate enable flag) into `VerifyConfig` (`[verify]`) while all other `overlap_*` keys go into `LLMConfig` (`[tool.rebar.llm]`), with no stated decision rule for which layer owns which flag. The Scope section says 'Only the gate ENABLE flag `overlap_enabled` lives in `VerifyConfig` `[verify]`' but gives no rationale, and the confirmed `VerifyConfig` dataclass already has analogous boolean flags (`enable_code_review`, `require_plan_review_for_claim`) that follow a consistent pattern — making the asymmetric placement a structural gap an executor cannot resolve without guessing.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Only the gate ENABLE flag `overlap_enabled` lives in `VerifyConfig` `[verify]`; the opportunistic hook attached to the same post-write path as push'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
